policy_statement: Fisheries adaptation strategy: strengthen capacity of fisheries authorities for research/monitoring/enforcement; support artisanal fishing communities with training, gear and alternative livelihoods; establish monitoring/reporting/verification and adaptive management; support research on climate pressures; identify/map/protect valuable fisheries areas including marine protected areas; reduce external stressors via regulatory measures; integrate fisheries into national adaptation and food security policies; establish early warning and information/communication networks; and regulate fishing to prevent overexploitation.
mechanism_description: The strategy strengthens fisheries governance (research/monitoring, early warning, protected areas, pollution controls, and regulated fishing practices) to reduce climate and non-climate stresses on marine ecosystems and sustain fish stocks and livelihoods under changing conditions.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard + ecosystem stress → fisheries management/adaptation actions → ↓ exposure/sensitivity + ↑ adaptive capacity → sustained fish stocks/livelihoods under climate change
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed human activity/system: Fisheries governance and practices are strengthened (monitoring and research, enforcement, adaptive harvest rules, protected areas, pollution/stressor controls, early warning systems, and livelihood support/diversification).  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced overfishing and non-climate stressors plus improved ecosystem stewardship increases stock health and reproductive capacity; early warning and information systems enable behavioral/operational adjustments to climate variability; spatial protections safeguard critical habitats and recruitment areas.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower climate risk to fisheries productivity and coastal livelihoods (greater resilience, fewer losses during climate shocks, improved food security and income stability).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced vulnerability of fisheries and fishing communities to climate-driven ecosystem change and shocks, sustaining livelihoods and food security.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Managing fisheries to cope with climate impacts primarily reduces vulnerability (stocks and communities) rather than directly reducing GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although ecosystem protection can incidentally support carbon sequestration and resource efficiency, the core intent and largest effect is maintaining fisheries function under climate change (adaptation/resilience).  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy within this mechanism were framed mainly around blue-carbon conservation (e.g., mangrove/seagrass carbon sinks) the dominant pathway could shift toward Nature-Based Solutions/mitigation; however, fisheries adaptation and governance measures generally remain adaptation-led.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that strengthens fisheries management specifically to sustain stocks/livelihoods amid changing conditions operates chiefly by reducing climate vulnerability and increasing adaptive capacity.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any mitigation, biodiversity, or efficiency co-benefits (e.g., reduced fuel use from operational changes, habitat carbon benefits) require policy-specific evidence and are not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential overlap with Nature-Based Solutions when fisheries resilience is pursued mainly via habitat restoration/protection explicitly for carbon sinks (blue carbon), and with Mitigation if measures target large reductions in fleet fuel use; absent those dominant framings, classification remains Adaptation.
key_indicators: “Adaptation strategies of the fisheries sector”; “Strengthen the capacity… for research monitoring and enforcement”; “Set up robust monitoring, reporting, and verification system”; “Identify, map, and protect areas valuable for fisheries… marine protected areas”; “regulatory measures to reduce… pollution… destructive fishing practices”; “Integrate fisheries fully into climate change adaptation and food security policies”; “establishment of early warning systems”; “Regulate fishing practices to prevent overexploitation”
co_benefits: Biodiversity conservation and habitat protection (marine protected areas); improved water quality (reducing land-based pollution); livelihoods and poverty reduction (training, alternative livelihoods); improved fisheries sustainability/stock health (overexploitation controls); public health benefits via reduced pollution.
instance_edge_case_notes: The excerpt bundles multiple instrument types (regulatory measures, investments/support, information systems). Classified by the dominant framing as a sector “adaptation strategy” (planning-strategy), though several bullets could individually be coded as regulatory-mandate, infrastructure-investment, or incentive-program.
